coredata.dc:title: Green approach to synthesize functional carbon nanoparticles at low temperature
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Carbajal Arízaga, Gregorio Guadalupe
Quiñones Galván, José Guadalupe
Bail, Alesandro
Pérez González, Andrea Lizeth
Pereyra Nuñez, Citlali
López Álvarez, Miguel Ángel
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles have demonstrated their potential to develop materials with advanced applications in which their luminescence and biocompatibility are exploited. In the search for sustainable methods to produce these nanoparticles, natural carbon sources such as plant- and animal-based products and by-products have been used. However, the existing procedures are still performed with high temperature, high pressure, and long reaction times. This report proposes a method to synthesize carbon nanoparticles using a tomato extract as the carbon source, followed by precipitation and calcination at a maximum of 60 °C under atmospheric pressure. This calcination temperature is the lowest reported and contributes to establishing a greener synthesis route. The detected fluorescence of these particles covers the entire region of the visible spectrum. The emission intensity is sensitive to zinc cations, demonstrating that this green method produces useful particles in detecting heavy metals similar to those reported by traditional methods. Furthermore, the aqueous solutions of these particles are photothermic when they are irradiated with red light, also showing their usefulness in biomedical developments. Therefore, this green synthesis at a very low temperature contributes to improving the green methods and boosts the sustainable development of advanced functional materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Carbon nanoparticle
Green synthesis
Low temperature
Fluorescence
Photothermal response

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction profile of carbonized samples obtained at 55 and 60 °C. Fluorescence of aqueous solutions of the powders irradiated with a UV laser.
Infrared spectra for powdered carbon samples obtained at 55 and 60 °C.
FE-SEM images of the carbon particles obtained by termal treatment at 55 and 60 °C.
Raman spectra of carbon nanoparticles obtained at 55 and 60 °C and the deconvoluted curves.
Particle size of carbon nanoparticles in aqueous dispersion. Each plot is the average result from three DLS measurements.
Photoluminescence spectrum of the carbon nanoparticles prepared at 55 and 60 °C and their deconvolution.
Photoluminescence spectra of CNP60 in aqueous solution exposed to different ZnCl2 concentrations and the intensity decay.
Increasing in the temperature of the CNP60 solutions irradiated by low-energy red light (640 nm).
TEM micrograph of the CNP60 sample with photothermal response.
Comparison of properties and application of carbon nanoparticles obtained from different sources.

row.entry: Fluorescence for in vivo microscopy imaging.

head.ce:title: Green approach to synthesize functional carbon nanoparticles at low temperature

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles have demonstrated their potential to develop materials with advanced applications in which their luminescence and biocompatibility are exploited. In the search for sustainable methods to produce these nanoparticles, natural carbon sources such as plant- and animal-based products and by-products have been used. However, the existing procedures are still performed with high temperature, high pressure, and long reaction times. This report proposes a method to synthesize carbon nanoparticles using a tomato extract as the carbon source, followed by precipitation and calcination at a maximum of 60 °C under atmospheric pressure. This calcination temperature is the lowest reported and contributes to establishing a greener synthesis route. The detected fluorescence of these particles covers the entire region of the visible spectrum. The emission intensity is sensitive to zinc cations, demonstrating that this green method produces useful particles in detecting heavy metals similar to those reported by traditional methods. Furthermore, the aqueous solutions of these particles are photothermic when they are irradiated with red light, also showing their usefulness in biomedical developments. Therefore, this green synthesis at a very low temperature contributes to improving the green methods and boosts the sustainable development of advanced functional materials.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new materials is needed to design devices with advanced functions and high performance. Some of the attractive properties are the significant strength and low density of carbon nanotubes, the electrical conductivity and catalytic activity of graphene, the strength of carbon fibers, and the photoluminescence of carbon dots.

Carbon dots (CD) attracted the attention of scientists and engineers due to their bright photoluminescence with a broad range of visible emissions. CDs are particles below 20 nm formed by a lattice with sp2/sp3 carbon hybridizations either with a crystalline graphite organization or amorphous [1].

Although carbon atoms form the main core, heteroatoms are also present, providing polarity, improving solubility, and modifying the energy of the molecular orbitals influencing the luminescence. The intensity and wavelength of the emission depend on the size, aggregation, and morphology of the particles. Therefore, carbon dots are excellent candidates for the preparation of catalysts [2], fluorescent inks for security applications [3], probes and fluorescent sensors to detect biomolecules like dopamine [4], volatile organic compounds [5], and also selective metals in water [6]; in fact, a simple setup of CD solutions with a commercial smartphone analyzes the fluorescent emission and is capable to selectively detect mercury among several other metals achieving the detection limit of 50 nM [7]. Advanced applications are also present, such as agents for intracellular imaging [8], detection of explosives [9], antimicrobial agents [9,10], and initiator of polymerization [11]. In addition, carbon dots are also attractive due to their low or null toxicity, compatibility with biological systems, and relatively low cost [1].

Procedures to prepare carbon dots require a carbon source and high temperature to complete the carbonization. The carbon source can also act as a functionalizing agent to avoid aggregation and regulate the physicochemical properties of the particles. Physical methods are mainly used to prepare carbon dots through the top-down route. For this, a carbon source like graphite, carbon soot, or carbon fibers is subjected to laser ablation, electrochemical peeling, or arc discharge. Although these methods require high energy consumption, they allow for high yields and large-scale production [1].

The chemical methods are easier to set up. Therefore, most of the articles reporting CDs involve chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal reactions, and microwave-assisted hydrolysis [1]. These procedures usually require a temperature above 150 °C for at least 3 h of reaction, which leads to a high energy consumption [2,7].

Despite the efficiency of the current synthesis methods, they are in continuous development to establish improvements to align them to routes with less environmental impact. Green chemistry is the discipline focused on studying and proposing methods covering the twelve principles of sustainability involving low-environmental impact reagents, less residue production, and low energy consumption [12].

Although several green chemistry approaches to prepare CD have been reported, the high temperature and extended reaction time are barriers that must be overcome to achieve a desirable green process, reduce cost limitations for scaling up, and comply with safety regulations with large volume reactors tolerating high pressures. For example, several biomass sources such as plant extracts, lignocellulosic residues, and animal wastes have been converted into CDs; however, the pyrolysis temperature treatments are between 180 and 350 °C for periods between 2 and 48 h [13,14]. A very recent review article focused on the green synthesis of carbon dots confirms the use of high temperature and large reaction times [13]. Specific cases are the carbon dots produced with acerola fruit (Malpighiaemarginata), obtained by heating in water as the continuous medium at 180 °C for 18 h [15].

The obtention of carbon nanoparticles with high nitrogen content has been obtained from seaweed extract at 160 °C for 120 h [3]; also, carbon dots from beetroot were produced at 150 °C for 16 h [16], and pomegranate extract at 150 °C for 16 h [17]. Also, banana and maple leaves pyrolyzed under a nitrogen atmosphere at 350 °C for 2 h produced these particles [14]. Other procedures incorporated a co-solvent, as in the case of the synthesis with lemon juice mixed with formamide heated at 200 °C for 6 h [18], or even the addition of inorganic salts to enhance the hydrolysis of disulfide bridges in sewage sludge by heating at 260 °C for 24 h [19].

This paper aims to contribute to a greener process in the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles, describing a procedure using a vegetable source of carbon (tomato extract) with mild heat (55 and 60 °C) and avoiding solvents. Further, we demonstrated that the obtained carbon nanoparticles are functional as a metal cations detector and photothermal active particles as desired for biomedical treatments.

Tomato samples (Lycopersicon esculentum) were purchased from a local market and washed with water. The peel and seeds were removed, and then the mass was ground in a pestle, filtered with a 3 mm mesh, and 40 mL of the liquid extract was transferred to a vessel for the reaction. Separately, a salts solution was prepared with 40 mL of water, 2.803 g of Mg(NO3)2•6H2O and 1.369 g of Al(NO3)3•9H2O to obtain a concentration of 0.274 M and 0.091 M, respectively, which was added to the tomato pulp, stirred and adjusted to pH 8.5 with 1 M NaOH solution under stirring. In this process, the magnesium and aluminum salts produce layered double hydroxide (LDH) particles, and their aggregation promotes the precipitation of solids in the pulp [20]. However, centrifugation was a key step in obtaining partial precipitation in this experiment. The suspension obtained was centrifuged in a Gerber Instruments Centrifuge (K. Schneider and Co. AG) operating at 1380 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was separated with a pipette, placed in porcelain dishes, and then introduced in the oven at 55 and 60 °C. After 24 h, the dishes containing solid carbon nanoparticles (CNP) were stored in a desiccator and labeled as CNP55 and CNP60.

The infrared spectra were collected in a Thermo Scientific spectrophotometer, model Nicolet iS5 iD5, using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. The powder sample was placed over an equipment window and measured with 16 scans of 4 cm−1 resolution. Micrographs were obtained in a field emission scanning electronic microscope (FE-SEM), model TESCAN MIRA3 LMU. Around 10 mg of sample was dispersed in 1 mL of water, and then one drop of the suspension was placed in the sample holder and then dried at 30 °C for 10 min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL equipment, model JEM-1010, operated with a voltage of 80 kV. X-ray diffraction profiles were recorded with a Panalytical equipment, model Empyream. The powder samples were deposited over glass support and pressed to form a smooth surface. The samples were measured with a step of 0.02° and a collection time of 30 s per step. Elemental analyses were conducted with 2 mg of the powder sample introduced in a LECO TruSpec Micro instrument. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were measured using a Malvern equipment, model Zetasizer Nano ZS. The laser wavelength for DLS measurements was 633 nm. The powder sample (10 mg) was dispersed in 25 mL of water, shaken by hand, and stored. The solution was analyzed 24 h later.

Raman spectroscopy was performed in an Acton SpectraPro 2755i spectrometer using an excitation line of 473 nm. A photomultiplier tube Hamamatsu R925 was used to detect the Raman signal of the light scattered upon contact with the solid sample supported onto a glass plate.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy was used to measure the fluorescence of the carbon nanoparticles and their response to chemical agents. The analysis was carried out using a xenon arc lamp of 1000 W, fixing the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The emitted light was collected by a photomultiplier tube Hamamatsu R925 coupled to an Acton SpectraPro 2755i spectrometer. The laser beam was focused on a quartz cuvette with CNP55 and CNP60 solutions, prepared by dispersing 400 mg of powdered sample in 25 mL of distilled water.

The fluorescence of carbon nanoparticles in solution was evaluated in the presence of ZnCl2 as follows: first, a solution of CNP60 was prepared by adding 0.200 g of CNP60 in 25 mL of deionized water. An aliquot of 2.00 mL of this carbon solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette. After that, a 1 M ZnCl2 aqueous solution was prepared and aliquots of 200, 400, 600, and 800 microliters were added to the CNP60 solution in the quartz cuvette. Before each measurement, the new solution was vigorously shaken to ensure homogeneity.

The photothermal assays were conducted with carbon nanoparticle suspensions prepared with 150, 300, and 600 mg of powder dissolved in 15 mL of water. The carbon particles were entirely dissolved by hand-shaking.

The solution was transferred to a 20 mL Petri dish with constant magnetic stirring to improve heat diffusion to any point in the solution. A digital thermometer was introduced in the solution. The LED was placed 2 cm away over the surface of the solution, ensuring radiation of 150 mW cm−2. The air condition system was set at 17 °C, which was the experiment's starting temperature. The heat was promoted with the LED irradiating a monochromatic light of 640 nm.

The products obtained after the thermal treatment in the oven showed a dark powder appearance that could be easily ground in a mortar. The XRD analysis was conducted to verify if any crystalline product was formed during the flocculation process. The diffraction profiles of CNP55 and CNP60 showed that the samples presented a wide signal in the 20–30° (2q) range, similar to that of a glass, which is typical of amorphous materials (Fig. 1 ); therefore, the liquid fraction was correctly separated from the precipitate formed y LDH and tomato pulp.

Considering that XRD analysis does not reveal the presence of CNP, fluorescence analyses were conducted to determine if graphitic carbon domains were present. The CNP55 and CNP60 aqueous solutions presented fluorescence by exposure to 360 nm light (Fig. 1). Therefore, these particles could be classified as belonging to the group of carbon dot polymers (CDP), which may have more amorphicity than other carbon nanoparticles such as carbon dots (CD) [1].

Samples were subjected to infrared spectroscopy analyses to identify the molecular fractions composing the powders. Data for CNP55 and CNP60 showed a similar profile of vibrational bands produced by functional groups containing carbon, such as the low-intensity shoulder centered at 2970 cm−1, which overlaps several symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes from H-C-H. The band at 1334 cm−1 has an intense shoulder at 1400 cm−1, which could be attached to a C=C vibrational mode [21] and a wide area of absorption below 650 cm−1, referring to =C-H vibrational modes [21].

Also, absorption bands match typical wavenumber values for COO− at 1332 and 1600 cm−1, suggesting the presence of the carboxylate group [22]. These two absorption bands of carboxylate are wide and might be overlapped with other signals; for instance, the absorption band at 1600 cm-1 may have contributions from the bending mode of the water molecule and N-H stretching modes. Furthermore, the band at 1330 cm−1 may show contributions from the C-H stretching mode. The presence of amine groups was identified by the band at 3200 cm−1 [22]. Both CNP55 and CNP60 have -OH surface groups and they were identified by their typical absorption bands at 3400 and 1070 cm−1 [22]. The observed bands agree with the formation of carbon nanoparticles containing both amino and carboxylate groups as in previous reports of carbon dots prepared from natural compounds [22]; these similarities corroborate the formation of carbon nanoparticles at 55 and 60 °C. According to the literature, tomato contains fiber composed of cellulose, hemicelluloses and pectins, lycopene as the main antioxidant pigment, monounsaturated fatty acids, minerals, phenolic compounds, ascorbic acid, and amino acids [23,24]. The precipitation process with LDH particles removes the pulp and the lycopene fraction was determined by infrared data and chromatography analyses [20]. Then, the aqueous fraction is more prone to contain ascorbic acid, phenolic compounds, and amino acids; in fact, the intense bands related to C-H, N-H and C-O fractions in the infrared spectra are in agreement with this content (Fig. 2 ).

The morphology of the samples was analyzed by FE-SEM. In this assay, no metallic coating was used in order to prevent the hiding of boundaries between grains (Fig. 3 ). Sample CNP55 was firstly analyzed at 20 K magnification, and it was observed it was formed by polyhedral particles larger than 1 micrometer. In its turn, the magnification above 50k times allowed the identification of well-defined edges of polyhedrons, which quickly decomposed during the focalization (indicated by the arrow).

The sample CNP60 was formed mainly by semi-spherical particles, and they did not deform with the electron beam. In addition, its conductivity allowed to achieve a magnification of 90 K and, consequently, the clear observation of particles smaller than 200 nm in size.

Raman spectroscopy analyses were conducted to determine vibrations of graphitic of graphite-disordered regions. Raman spectra for CNP55 and CNP60 (Fig. 4 ) present the same profile, except for a slight difference in the full width at half maximum of the band (FWHM) centered at 1500 cm−1. This signal is unique and very wide, like those observed for carbon particles synthesized from natural products [25].

It is expected that carbon particles composed of graphene fragments present bands from defective regions (D) and ordered graphene (G) at 1620 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. Considering this, a deconvolution procedure was performed to estimate the contribution of each component and compare the relative intensity (ID/IG), which was used to establish a relationship with the irregularities or vacancies of sp3 carbon [25]. The CNP55 sample presented ID/IG = 1.0, while CNP60 showed ID/IG = 0.9, indicating that the higher temperature favored the organization of the graphene network. This fact can also be observed with the areas of the deconvoluted spectra, in which the G signal, corresponding to the ordered material [26], area changes from 48 to 52% when the temperature was increased from 55 to 60 °C. Consequently, the semi-spherical particles observed by FE-SEM had a higher number of ordered atoms.

DLS and zeta potential analyses were conducted to determine the hydrodynamic size of the particles and their aggregation. The powdered CNPs were easily dispersed in water, and their solutions were used to measure the particle size. Fig. 5 presents the hydrodynamic size centered at 590 and 450 nm for CNP55 and CNP60, respectively. This change indicates a decrease in the size of the aggregates formed in water when the temperature increased to 60 °C. The aggregation could be associated with the low zeta potential values of both samples, being −12.3 mV (S.D. = 1.1) for CNP55 and −18.5 mV (S.D. = 1.4) for CNP60. The slightly higher value in the CNP60 sample, indicating more repulsion between particles, agrees with the narrower and smaller size curve in the DLS histogram. Then, the higher temperature probably degrades cationic groups of the particles like amine groups and favors the prevalence of anions, mainly carboxylate groups. In fact, other researchers have observed lower or more positive zeta potential values with higher ammine concentration [9]. The consumption of amine groups could be related to the slight reduction in nitrogen as detected in the elemental analyses where CNP55 presented 9.3% N, 38.6% C, and 3.6% H, and CNP60, 8.7% N, 30.0% C, and 3.4% H. The low carbon content has also been observed in carbon nanoparticles prepared with chitosan [27].

Although both samples also presented aggregates larger than 5 micrometers, their area contribution is quite low. The aggregates mentioned here refer to groups of the particles observed by FE-SEM. Although hydrodynamic size control is relevant and should be monitored when biomedical applications are desired, the size of the samples CNP55 and CNP60 has no drawbacks in developing sensors or optical devices where fluorescence is exploited.

The photoluminescence analyses were conducted to identify the light emission of carbon nanoparticles. The spectra for both powdered samples are composed by a wide range of emissions in the visible region (Fig. 6 ). The spectra were deconvoluted by fixing the components with the wavelengths where incipient shoulders appear, namely green (539), yellow (583 nm), orange (626 nm) and red (690 nm). Although the most intense emission occurs in the green-yellow range, a significant contribution of the orange-red range was noticed. The maximum intensity was reached at 583 nm in CNP55 and 544 nm in CNP60. It has been studied that green emission is associated with energetic transitions in graphitic nanodomains [15], but in addition, it is also influenced by surface states that depend on the composition of the particles; therefore, a high population of carboxylate and carbonyl groups generates the green emission [16]. The presence of these carbonyl groups was confirmed with the infrared spectrum. In this sense, the contribution of the red emission (red-shift) is also given by a high degree of oxidation on the surface of the particles in agreement with the tomato components having a large population of oxygen.

Regarding the CNP60 spectrum, the emission in the green-yellow range increases, and the area of ​​the red-orange emission falls, so it can be considered that higher temperature contributes to more effective carbonization due to higher loss of oxygen.

Once it was shown that there was the formation of fluorescent particles at low temperature, the sensitivity to detect zinc cations was monitored in order to evaluate its usefulness as a detection agent, as it occurs with carbon nanoparticles conventionally prepared at high temperature. The CNP60 sample was chosen for this experiment because its fluorescence spectrum was the narrowest.

Fig. 7 a depicts the spectra in which the highest intensity is presented by the solution of pure CPN60 in distilled water. When adding 200 to 600 µL of the ZnCl2 solution, the intensity decreased proportionally, and this inverse dependence with the salt concentration is demonstrated with the negative slope of Fig. 7b. The plot follows a linear trend up to 600 µL (dotted zone in Fig. 7b), indicating that the carbon nanoparticles obtained at low temperature are promising for further studies as probes in detecting metals in water, as has been done with carbon dots prepared at high temperature [7,28]. However, further studies are needed to verify the selectivity and rages of linear response.

Another assessment to probe the usefulness of the green synthesis at low temperature was conducted with CNP60 to compare with previous reports of carbon nanoparticles obtained by traditional methods [29].

The measurement in water as blank, recorded an increment in temperature from 22 to 23 °C in 600 s of irradiation (Fig. 8 ); a fall of the final point was observed since the air condition temperature in the laboratory was 17 °C, and it cooled down the solution. The addition of 150 mg in 15 mL of water increased the final temperature to 24 °C, proving that the presence of CNP60 was responsible for the photothermal effect. The addition of 300 mg of CNP60 intensified the heating, reaching 26 °C after 600 s of irradiation, which was the same effect observed for that obtained with 600 mg solution. Then a high-concentrated solution is not needed to achieve higher efficiency. The thermal response is close to recent measurements conducted under similar conditions, especially at low-power radiations; for instance, copper-CD assemblies irradiated with 600 mW cm−2 increased 15 °C [30], and 5 °C with 400 mW cm−2 [31]. This result suggests that the carbon particles obtained with green reagents at low temperature are promising to develop carbon particles for photothermal therapy. As it is known, the thermal activity and fluorescence are produced by nanodomains of carbon, and these domains are observed in Fig. 9 . Then our procedure is a suitable alternative to prepare carbonaceous materials with low energy with possible relevant application when compared with other particles obtained by traditional high temperature methods as exemplified in Table 1 .

The carbon source for the top-down synthesis of the carbon nanoparticles here reported was the supernatant fraction formed after precipitating the tomato pulp with LDH. The reagents to induce such precipitation are nontoxic; in fact, LDH are widely used as materials in biomedical and food applications [32,33]. Although the precipitated fraction was not used for the experiments in this article, it is not considered as residue since it is prepared with generally recognized as safe (GRAS) reagents [20], and the solid is even useful as a nutraceutical. Then the reagents used in these experiments reduce the environmental impact. The low temperature applied to transform the supernatant into carbon nanoparticles overcomes the excessive energy consumed in the typical process of preparing carbon nanoparticles.

The carbon nanoparticles were prepared using tomato pulp, generally recognized as safe reagents, with a maximal temperature of 60 °0C and atmospheric pressure. These conditions address the principles of green chemistry, specifically the use of low-environmental impact reagents, low residues formation, and low energy consumption.

The obtained carbon nanoparticles presented some of the properties reported for carbon nanoparticles obtained by conventional methods such as the fluorescent response against metal cations and the photothermal activity against infrared irradiation, opening a promising way to prepare biomedical green carbon particles.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The corresponding author certifies that there is no conflict of interest in relation to this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was suppported by the Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología (CONACyT) project 256690 (Fondo Sectorial de Investigación para la Educación). A.L.P.G. and C.P.N. thank the scholarship provided by CONACyT.
